relay_password.title: RELAY ACCESS
relay_password.description: Access code required. Use the active relay authorization, not the deprecated fragment.

power_routing_01.title: RELAY POWER ROUTING
power_routing_01.description: Match unstable power nodes with the correct station systems.

events.text: [UNKNOWN RELAY SIGNAL DETECTED]
Can you hear me?
No. Of course not.
That would be too convenient, and apparently convenience was not included in this station's design philosophy.
Please don't be another automated relay echo. I have been losing arguments with machines for weeks.
Wait.
You answered.
Okay. That's real. You're real.
Sorry. I had a better opening line planned.
It was something professional. Calm. Very reassuring.
Then a human being actually answered me and my brain threw it directly into a fire.
I'm Dr. Ethan Hale. Chief Engineer, Orbital Research Station ECHO.
And before you ask, no, my official emergency plan was not supposed to be begging a stranger through a half-dead relay.
Although, in fairness, the official emergency plan also assumed the emergency systems would work.
So we were all being optimistic.
Right. Names first. Panic second.
I kept the relay spine alive after the blackout. Which sounds heroic until you remember the relay is currently coughing blood.
Chief Engineer means I was responsible for keeping things running.
Which is hilarious in retrospect.
I once wrote a sixteen-page maintenance warning about this relay.
Management scheduled a meeting to discuss the warning.
Never trust anyone who schedules a meeting about an alarm.
Seriously?
No. That's fair. Annoying, but fair.
Oxygen is at thirty-two percent. Suit battery says forty, which means twenty if it's feeling honest.
My left glove has a pressure leak that I am choosing to call 'minor' because morale is a fragile animal.
[BIOMETRIC FRAGMENT ATTACHED]

events.title: BIOMETRIC FRAGMENT: HALE
events.content:
SUBJECT: ETHAN HALE
HEART RATE: ELEVATED
OXYGEN: 32%
SUIT LINK: UNSTABLE
VOICEPRINT MATCH: 81%
events.caption: Partial telemetry fragment transmitted through the relay.

events.text: There. If you are a rescue protocol, that should satisfy you.
If you are not, please pretend to be impressed.
Something happened here. Something very wrong.
The station went dark. Comms folded. Internal routing broke into pieces.
You're the first signal I've had in months that didn't sound like a ghost wearing someone else's mouth.
I know how that sounds.
Unfortunately, I have had a lot of time to workshop terrifying descriptions.
Most of them are about the ventilation system. It whistles when the pressure drops.
It is not a comforting whistle.
Good. I needed to hear that more than I want to admit.
But don't confuse helping me with trusting me. Those are different wires. Cross them and something sparks.
That was not me being mysterious.
That was an engineer begging you not to connect unknown leads because they are emotionally compelling.
Smart. Slower, but smart.
Ask questions. Just don't take forever. The station has a habit of punishing hesitation.
It also punishes confidence, shortcuts, curiosity, and occasionally breathing too loudly.
So, you know. Balanced design.
I'm sending the only crew record I can still access.
If it cuts out halfway, blame the relay. Or the corpse of the relay. It was never very good at dying gracefully.
That code should still open the relay door, but the file is damaged.
You'll need to read it properly. Current authorization, not deprecated fragment.
This is usually the part where I would say something reassuring.
But I once locked myself out of this same relay room with a full access badge.
So let's agree I am more of a cautionary tale than a mentor.

choices.text: CAN YOU JUST TELL ME THE CODE?

events.text: I would love to. Truly. Unfortunately, my memory currently has the structural integrity of wet paper.
The log says active authorization starts with seventy-three and ends with one. The old fragment has a nine in it.
I can feel the answer standing just outside my head, which is very rude of it.
[HALE COOPERATION DEGRADED]
The relay room should still be operational.
Should.
I hate that word now.
Before the blackout, I used to complain about normal things.
Bad coffee. Loose panels. Patel stealing my torque wrench and denying it for three weeks.
I miss problems that could be solved with yelling and a screwdriver.
[LOCKED DEVICE DETECTED: RELAY ACCESS]
[ACCESS DENIED]
Wrong code. Don't panic. Panic is how machines convince humans to make a second worse decision.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: The panel noticed. Of course it noticed. It has one job and apparently it's good at being offended.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I once watched this lock reject a valid badge because the badge was 'visually suspicious.'
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Try again. Active relay authorization. Not the deprecated fragment.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: [SECURITY ATTENTION INCREASED]
[ACCESS GRANTED]
Good. Either you're very smart, or I accidentally taught a stranger how to break into my station.
Let's pretend it's the first one.
Also, for the record, if you ever use this skill criminally, I had no part in it.
[RELAY SPINE POWER UNSTABLE]
Door's open, but the relay spine is starving. I need you to route power before this link collapses again.
And yes, I know that asking a stranger to reroute life-support-adjacent power is a lot for a first conversation.
I am choosing to call this team-building.
[POWER ROUTING FAILED]
No, no— don't feed the relay from reserve. Life support gets reserve. Relay gets the unstable node because apparently everything today is a moral compromise.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I realize that sounds insane.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Unfortunately, the station was designed by people who believed labels were a substitute for judgment.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Reset the board. Slowly. If the relay screams, let it scream. Life support gets first rights to anything clean.
events.speaker: HALE

choices.text: ASK HALE TO EXPLAIN THE ROUTING.

events.text: [POWER ROUTING ACCEPTED]

events.title: RELAY REPAIR
events.subtitle: Stabilizing relay spine and restoring signal carrier

events.text: There. That's better.
Still terrible. But now it's professionally terrible.
I used to say that about our coffee.
No one believed me, but bad coffee is an important morale system.
When the coffee went, civilization went with it.
I was hoping you didn't notice that.
Which is a stupid hope, since you are apparently the only competent thing connected to this relay.
Something is listening through the carrier. Not a person. Not exactly.
At first I thought it was a diagnostic loop.
Then it started waiting for me to stop talking before it answered.
Loops don't learn manners.
Good. One nightmare at a time.
I can work with one nightmare. I have a system.
The system is mostly denial and moving quickly.
Very advanced.
Hold on.
Did you hear that?

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: NOT FIRST CONTACT
Ignore that.
No. Sorry. That's a stupid thing to say.
Don't ignore it. Just don't obey it without thinking.
There's a difference between listening and handing something the steering wheel.
I learned that late.

choices.text: ARE YOU SCARED OF THAT VOICE?

events.text: Door 12?
You heard a voice from nowhere and immediately handed it the steering wheel.
Fine. If it wants Door 12, we check Door 12. But we do it with records, not obedience.
I need you to understand something before we open anything with that name attached.
Door 12 is not just a door.
I hate that I just said that.
[HALE TRUST DEGRADED]
Good. Records first. Voices second.
That is the closest thing to a survival philosophy I have left.
If the records are lying, at least they have to lie in order.
Voices don't bother with order.
[SECURITY CACHE RECOVERED]

events.title: SECURITY LOG
events.content:
21:17 — Door 12 opened.
21:17 — Manual override accepted.
21:18 — Operator identity redacted.
21:20 — Internal sensor flagged decompression risk.
events.caption: Recovered security event from the station archive.

events.text: [MEDICAL CACHE RECOVERED]

events.title: MEDICAL REPORT
events.content:
21:20 — Casualty reported.
Cause: decompression trauma.
Location: Door 12 access corridor.
Status: fatal.
events.caption: Recovered emergency medical report.

events.title: CAMERA ANALYSIS REPORT
events.content:
SOURCE: DOOR 12 SECURITY FEED
FRAME STATUS: VISUAL DATA CORRUPTED
ANALYSIS RESULT:
- One subject detected outside Door 12.
- Subject biometric outline matches Ethan Hale.
- Object detected in subject's right hand: manual override handle.
- Frame terminated before decompression event.
events.caption: Automated analysis generated from corrupted visual feed. No image recovered.
events.message: [CAMERA ANALYSIS REPORT ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

events.text: That is a lot of evidence for one very small corridor.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Manual override. Decompression risk. Medical report.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: It reads like a machine trying to describe a scream.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I don't like that my outline is in the camera analysis.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I especially don't like that I can't call it wrong.
events.speaker: HALE

choices.text: DO YOU REMEMBER ANY OF THIS?

events.text: I can't explain evidence I don't remember making.
And every time you say my name like a charge being read in court, it gets harder to think.
But I get it.
If I were on your side of the screen, I wouldn't trust me either.
That is not comforting. I heard it after I said it.
[HALE COOPERATION DEGRADED]
Pieces.
A warning alarm. Door 12. Someone shouting my name like I had already done something unforgivable.
I remember my hand on the override handle.
Not pulling it. Not yet. Just holding it.
Then nothing.
No, not nothing.
That's too clean. This feels like a page torn out and left somewhere nearby.
Operator?
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: If you are still there, save the records.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Not because I trust them.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: Because I don't trust myself without them.
events.speaker: HALE

choices.text: KEEP THE CHANNEL OPEN A LITTLE LONGER.

events.text: Look at the damaged authorization like a bad sentence, not a locked door.
Active record: seventy-three, blank, one. Deprecated fragment: three-one-nine. The system warns you not to use the whole old fragment. It doesn't say every piece of it is useless.
This is the kind of puzzle the archive designers loved.
They thought it made people feel clever. Mostly it made people hate archive designers.
So we borrow the only digit that fits and hope the relay is less vindictive than it looks.
[RELAY PANEL AWAITING INPUT]
Archive cache can survive on the stable low draw. It doesn't need heroics, just patience.
Life support gets emergency reserve because I am annoyingly attached to breathing.
The relay spine takes the unstable high draw. Dangerous, ugly, but enough to push our signal through.
That is engineering in one sentence, by the way: dangerous, ugly, but enough.
[ROUTING PANEL RESET]
[RECORD PACKAGE SEALED]
Good. Put the evidence somewhere the station can't chew on it.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: And Operator? If Door 12 comes up again, don't let me dodge it. I don't trust my memory enough to protect you from it.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I want to say I wouldn't hurt anyone.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: I hate that the best I can do is say I don't remember hurting anyone.
events.speaker: HALE
UNKNOWN
events.text: HE OPENED IT
[CHANNEL HELD OPEN]

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: DO NOT LET HIM CLOSE THE RECORD
You kept the channel open. Brave, reckless, or both. Usually those die in the same corridor.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: That was a joke.
events.speaker: HALE

events.text: No it wasn't.
events.speaker: HALE
UNKNOWN
events.text: ASK WHO WAS OUTSIDE

events.speaker: UNKNOWN
events.text: NOT FIRST CONTACT
That is a very simple question with a very bad answer.
I have not heard another living voice in...
No.
Let's not count it. Counting makes things official.
There are sounds. Pumps, relays, metal cooling in the walls.
Sometimes the station shifts and I almost convince myself someone is walking nearby.
It never is.
So yes. I think I am alone.
And no, I am not enjoying the character development.
Yes.
That was too fast, wasn't it?
I should have said something brave and technically useless.
But yes. I am scared of it.
Not because it sounds monstrous.
Because sometimes it sounds like it knows where the missing seconds went.
And I am not sure I want them back.

focus: Hale's first contact made more human, lonely, and conversational
Added idle dialogue, jokes, complaints, and normal-life references
Made UNKNOWN more cryptic and less instructional
Preserved puzzle IDs, success/failure flow, and episode transition structure
Expanded emotional reactions after major evidence discoveries
